article.title: GitHub Repository Graphs as Auditable Feed-Forward Networks for Coding Agent Reasoning

section.title: Abstract
section.para: Feed-forward network (FFN) layers in transformer language models act as key-value memories: each neuron encodes a key vector (gate) that activates on specific input patterns and a value vector (down-projection) that injects factual tokens into the residual stream geva2021ffn. This factual storage is opaque — there is no principled path from a model output back to the specific FFN weight that produced it.

We present an architecture in which a GitHub repository's static call graph, dependency graph, and environment-variable seam graph are encoded as a vector-indexed triple store and substituted for the FFN layer during inference via the larql WalkFfn gate-KNN mechanism larql2024. The substitution makes every factual retrieval step inspectable: the querying entity, the activated layer and feature slot, the gate similarity score, and the target token are all externally observable. This auditability is the primary value of the architecture over opaque FFN weights or traditional retrieval-augmented generation (RAG).

We report a critical batch-insert collision bug in the larql-python bindings (VectorIndex.find_free_feature), document its root cause (mmap/heap split in the Rust backend), present the fix (pre-scan eviction order), and propose a dual-theory experimental design whose observable divergence settles whether the WalkFfn mechanism encodes weighted BFS structure (Theory A) or global Laplacian spectral structure (Theory B).

section.title: Introduction: The FFN Opacity Problem
section.para: Geva et al. geva2021ffn showed that transformer FFN layers function as key-value memories: the up-projection weight matrix stores keys, and the down-projection matrix stores values. Meng et al. meng2022rome demonstrated that factual associations are localised to specific FFN layers and can be edited by rank-one weight updates (ROME). Both results establish that FFN weights store knowledge — but neither makes that storage auditable in the sense of tracing a specific output token back to the specific weight entry that produced it.

For coding agents — language model systems that write, read, and execute code — this opacity creates a verification problem. When an agent invokes a function, imports a module, or reads an environment variable, one cannot determine whether that decision derives from (a) training data about the function, (b) in-context examples, or (c) a hallucinated association with no grounding. The FFN weights are the primary candidate for (c), and they are unauditable.

The architecture described in this paper replaces (or supplements) the FFN layer with an externally-stored triple store derived from the target repository's actual call graph. Every retrieval from this store is fully observable: the querying entity, the activated feature, and the target are logged as structured data rather than absorbed into weight matrices.

section.title: Background: WalkFfn and the larql Knowledge Graph Engine
larql
section.para: larql is a knowledge graph inference engine that treats a pre-trained language model's weight matrices as the underlying embedding and retrieval infrastructure larql2024. It exposes two query modes:

listitem.para: WALK — a pure gate-vector scan over overrides_gate. Given a query entity, larql computes the entity's embedding, performs a cosine-similarity search over the gate vectors stored in the vindex, and returns the top-k feature slots with their similarity scores and associated target tokens. WALK does not perform a forward pass through the model weights.
INFER — a full forward pass followed by a KNN override at the post-logits layer. INFER reads from the knn_store (not overrides_gate) and requires loading model weights into memory.

section.para: The two query modes read from distinct stores and are not interchangeable. Inserts via vindex.insert() write to overrides_gate (WALK-visible). Inserts via INSERT INTO EDGES … MODE KNN write to knn_store (INFER-visible only).

section.title: WalkFfn Gate-KNN
section.para: The WalkFfn mechanism encodes a fact triple (entity, relation, target) as follows:

listitem.para: Compute the entity's embedding vector e = embed(entity).
Compute the relation's cluster centre c = cluster_centre(relation).
Synthesise a gate vector: g = normalise(e × 0.7 + c × 0.3), scaled to match the average L2 norm of existing gate vectors at the target layer.
Write g to the gate slot for feature f at layer l: vindex.set_gate_vector(l, f, g).
Write the target token and a confidence score: vindex.set_feature_meta(l, f, target, c_score=1.0).

section.para: At query time, WALK "entity" TOP k computes embed(entity) and returns the k feature slots whose gate vectors have the highest cosine similarity. The returned slots expose their layer index, feature index, gate score, and stored target token — all externally observable.

section.title: Vindex Layer Patches (.vlp)
section.para: A .vlp file is a JSON overlay that specifies a set of PatchOp operations to apply on top of a base vindex without modifying the base on disk. Each insert operation carries: op (either "insert" for WALK-visible or "insert_knn" for INFER-only), layer, feature, gate_vector_b64 (little-endian f32 array, base64-encoded), and down_meta with compact serde keys "t" (top_token), "i" (top_token_id), "c" (c_score).

The patch is applied in-memory via APPLY PATCH "path.vlp" in an LQL session. The base vindex on disk is never modified. This overlay design is what makes repository-specific knowledge both injectable and reversible.

section.title: Architecture: GitHub Repos as Auditable FFN
Triple Extraction
section.para: Repository knowledge is extracted as a typed triple store using static analysis of bash, Python, and Rust source files. The extraction pipeline (scripts/extract-graph.py, scripts/github_graph.py) produces triples of the form (entity, relation, target) where relation is one of: calls, sources, invokes, sets_env, reads_env, depends_on, tests, is_a, contains.
For the babel-harness repository at commit 94485d4, this yields 80 entities and 183 edges across the following node types:

section.title: Patch Builder: Vindexfile Form 1 → .vlp
section.para: The extraction pipeline emits a Vindexfile containing Form 1 INSERT directives:
scripts/build_repo_patch.py reads these directives and translates them into a .vlp patch file via vindex.insert()-equivalent logic. The key design decision is the write path:

listitem.para: Rejected: MODE KNN — INSERT INTO EDGES … MODE KNN writes to knn_store. The coding agent and bridge consumer both use WALK / entity_walk(), which does not read knn_store. KNN inserts are invisible to the primary consumer.

Adopted: vindex.insert() write path — writing gate vectors to overrides_gate via set_gate_vector() and set_feature_meta() produces op: "insert" operations in the .vlp that are visible to WALK queries. This enables the coding agent's retrieval to be fully observed.

section.title: Bridge Server
section.para: scripts/github_lql_bridge.py is a FastAPI server implementing the larql-server HTTP protocol (/v1/walk, /v1/stats, /v1/describe, /v1/relations, /v1/select). At startup it builds two separate vindex objects from the extracted triple store — one using topological layer assignment (Theory A) and one using spectral layer assignment (Theory B) — and on every /v1/walk call logs the divergence between the two theories' WALK outputs. The coding agent points its OPENAI_BASE_URL at this bridge, transparently using the repository graph as its knowledge base.

section.title: The Auditability Mechanism
section.para: The central claim of this architecture is that it makes coding agent reasoning auditable. We define auditability operationally: given a specific agent output token or decision, an auditor can identify the specific stored fact that produced it, verify that fact against the repository's source code, and determine whether the agent's reasoning was grounded in actual repository structure or hallucinated.

Each WALK query returns a list of (layer, feature, gate_score, top_token) tuples. Because the .vlp records which (entity, relation, target) triple was inserted at each (layer, feature) slot, the audit trail is complete:
section.programlisting: WALK "pi-harness" TOP 5 → L19:F306 gate=+12.2 top="curl" ← (pi-harness, invokes, curl) L19:F293 gate=+12.2 top="library" ← (pi-harness, is_a, library) L19:F2320 gate=+12.2 top="OPENROUTER…" ← (pi-harness, sets_env, OPENROUTER_CHECK_URL)
section.para: Each returned slot maps to a specific triple inserted from the repository graph. The auditor can verify: does bin/pi-harness actually invoke curl? Does it actually set OPENROUTER_CHECK_URL? These are answerable by reading the source file — no inference required.

This contrasts with both (a) opaque neural FFN weights, where the source of a token prediction cannot be attributed to a specific weight entry, and (b) traditional RAG, where retrieved passages are injected into the context window unstructured and their contribution to the output cannot be isolated.

section.title: F1: VectorIndex.find_free_feature() mmap/heap Split Bug
section.para: Finding: When vindex.insert() is called multiple times within a single Python process, all inserts after the first are silently directed to the same (layer, feature) slot, each evicting the previous insert. Only the last insert survives.

Root cause (source-confirmed): VectorIndex.find_free_feature() (larql-vindex/src/index/mutate/mod.rs:152–200) reads slot occupancy from self.metadata.down_meta_mmap (memory-mapped on-disk data). set_feature_meta() writes to self.metadata.down_meta (heap). The mmap is never updated by in-process writes. Consequently, on a fully-occupied layer, every call to find_free_feature() returns the same weakest slot (lowest c_score) because the mmap always reads the pre-write state.

The PatchedVindex overlay implementation in larql-vindex/src/patch/overlay.rs:335–381 explicitly documents this behaviour at line 338: find_free_feature keeps returning the same 'weakest' slot across calls — which is catastrophic for multi-fact INSERT. PatchedVindex.find_free_feature() correctly tracks overlay-claimed slots to avoid this; the larql-python insert() binding calls the base version and does not.

Fix: Pre-scan all N feature slots at the target layer before any writes, when the heap is empty and the mmap is ground truth. Build an ordered iterator: free slots first, then occupied slots ordered by c_score ascending (weakest eviction candidates first). Consume one slot per insert from this pre-built iterator. This mirrors PatchedVindex.find_free_feature().

Blast radius: smollm2-360m Layer 19 is fully occupied (all 2,560 feature slots have metadata). The fix therefore evicts the 95 weakest base features in the overlay (in-process only; the base vindex on disk is unmodified). The weakest evicted feature had token='Tro', c_score=1.2936.

section.title: F2: Layer Saturation in smollm2-360m
section.para: Layer 19 of the smollm2-360m base vindex has all 2,560 feature slots occupied (feature_meta(19, f) is not None for all f in 0..2559). This is the typical layer for the "calls" relation as determined by vindex.typical_layer("calls"). The pre-scan fix handles this correctly by treating the full layer as a pool of eviction candidates rather than reporting exhaustion.

section.title: F3: Gate Vector Synthesis Replication for Batch Inserts
section.para: Because vindex.insert() is broken for batch use (F1), build_repo_patch.py replicates its gate vector synthesis via the low-level set_gate_vector() / set_feature_meta() API. The replication mirrors larql-python/src/vindex.rs:971–1007 exactly: gate_vec = normalise(entity_embed × 0.7 + cluster_centre × 0.3), scaled to the average L2 norm of the first 100 gate vectors at the target layer.

Caveat: The normalization samples the first 100 features from the heap-after-promotion state. If any evicted slot index is below 100, the normalization sample shifts between inserts. This is a minor fidelity issue; vindex.insert() itself has the same property.

section.title: Dual-Theory Experimental Design
section.para: The scripts/graph_vindex.py module implements two formally-derived layer assignment strategies for the triple store. Their divergence on WalkFfn output is the primary experimental observable that can distinguish between two competing hypotheses about what WalkFfn encodes.

section.title: Theory A: Topological Layer Assignment (WalkFfn ≡ Weighted BFS)
section.para: The hypothesis is that the larql WalkFfn gate-KNN mechanism is equivalent to a weighted breadth-first search from a seed entity. Under this theory, the correct layer for an entity is its BFS hop-distance from the repository root, normalised to [0, num_layers):
section.programlisting: l(v) = floor(d(v, seed) / D * num_layers) # clipped to [0, num_layers-1] l(r) = round(mean((l(u) + l(v)) / 2 for (u,r,v) in E_r))
section.para: Unreachable nodes receive the fallback layer num_layers // 2.

section.title: Theory B: Spectral Layer Assignment (WalkFfn Encodes Global Spectral Structure)
section.para: The hypothesis is that WalkFfn encodes the global spectral structure of the code graph: entities whose call patterns are globally similar map to the same layer. Under this theory, the correct layer is the Laplacian spectral band that contains the most energy for the entity's eigenvector projection:
section.programlisting: l(v) = argmax_l Σ_{λ_i ∈ B_l} |x̂_v(i)|² # where B_l partitions [λ_min, λ_max] into num_layers bands # x̂_v is the v-th row of the eigenvector matrix of the normalised Laplacian

section.title: Observable: WalkFfn Output Divergence
section.para: The bridge server (scripts/github_lql_bridge.py) builds two independent vindex objects from the same triple store, one per theory, and logs the divergence between their WALK outputs on every /v1/walk call. A high divergence rate favours Theory B (spectral structure is globally meaningful and differs from BFS distance); a low divergence rate favours Theory A (BFS distance captures the same structure that WalkFfn would assign naturally).

section.title: Related Work
section.para: Geva et al. geva2021ffn established the key-value memory interpretation of FFN layers. Meng et al. meng2022rome demonstrated that factual associations are localised to specific FFN layers and are editable via rank-one weight updates. Both lines of work treat the FFN as the primary locus of factual knowledge in a transformer, motivating the approach of substituting a transparent external store.

Lewis et al. lewis2020rag introduced retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) as a way to ground language model outputs in external documents. The present architecture differs from RAG in two respects: (a) retrieved knowledge is injected via the FFN gate-KNN path rather than the context window, preserving the model's existing inference structure; and (b) retrieval is mediated by the model's own embedding space rather than a separate dense retrieval index, making the similarity judgements interpretable in terms of the model's prior knowledge.

Hopfield hopfield1982 established the theoretical foundation for associative memory in neural networks. The larql WalkFfn mechanism can be understood as a Hopfield-style associative memory operating on the model's gate vectors: each inserted triple constitutes a memory pattern, and a query entity's embedding is the retrieval cue.

section.title: Conclusions
section.para: We have presented an architecture in which a GitHub repository's call graph, dependency graph, and environment-seam graph are encoded as a vector-indexed triple store and injected into a language model's FFN layer via the larql WalkFfn gate-KNN mechanism. The result is a coding agent whose factual associations are fully auditable: every retrieval step exposes the querying entity, the activated layer and feature, the gate similarity score, and the target token — all traceable back to a specific line in the repository's source code.

We have reported a critical batch-insert collision bug in the larql-python bindings (F1), confirmed that Layer 19 of smollm2-360m is fully occupied (F2), and documented the gate vector synthesis replication required to work around the bug (F3). A pre-scan eviction order fix resolves the collision bug; the 95-triple babel-harness graph inserts correctly with 95 distinct feature slots and all targets retrievable via WALK.

The dual-theory experimental design (topological vs. spectral layer assignment) provides a concrete empirical path to settle whether the WalkFfn mechanism is semantically equivalent to weighted BFS or encodes global spectral graph structure. This question has implications for how future auditable-FFN architectures should assign knowledge to model layers.

bibliodiv.bibliomixed: Geva, M., Schuster, R., Berant, J., & Levy, O. (2021). Transformer Feed-Forward Layers Are Key-Value Memories. Proceedings of EMNLP 2021.
Meng, K., Bau, D., Andonian, A., & Belinkov, Y. (2022). Locating and Editing Factual Associations in GPT. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 35 (NeurIPS 2022).

Lewis, P., Perez, E., Piktus, A., Petroni, F., Karpukhin, V., Goyal, N., … Kiela, D. (2020). Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 33 (NeurIPS 2020).
Hopfield, J. J. (1982). Neural networks and physical systems with emergent collective computational abilities. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 79(8), 2554–2558.
Hayuk, C. (2024). larql: A knowledge graph inference engine for language models. GitHub repository: chrishayuk/larql.
